title: Currency Exchange in Berlin - Quick Summary
items: **The honest headline: most visitors do not need to exchange cash at all.** Berlin runs on euros, cards work in most hotels, restaurants and shops, and the cheapest euros in the city come from a bank ATM, not from an exchange counter.

**The airport and the tourist ATMs are where you lose money.** The exchange desks inside BER (Travelex, Euro Change) and the bright standalone **Euronet machines** near the Brandenburg Gate and big stations give some of the worst rates in Berlin. A "0% commission" sign just means the margin is hidden in the rate.

**One rule saves more than everything else: always pay in euros.** When an ATM or card terminal asks whether to charge you in euros or in your home currency, choose **euros (EUR)**. That home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion, and it can quietly cost you **5 to 13 percent** extra.

**Best way to get cash: a bank-run ATM with conversion declined.** Use a machine attached to a real German bank (Sparkasse, Volksbank, Deutsche Bank, Postbank). A fee-free travel card such as **Wise or Revolut** gets you very close to the real rate.

**If you genuinely need to change cash,** the Reisebank and Euro Change desks at **Hauptbahnhof, Friedrichstraße, Zoo and Alexanderplatz** are better than the airport and open long hours. Compare the actual buy rate, not the commission sign.

**Best move:** bring one card with no foreign-transaction fee, pull about **€100-200** from a bank ATM for Spätis, markets and cash-only bars, and pay by card everywhere else. Skip the exchange office entirely.
